task_name: Not applicable - FLS program overview presentation

frame_analyses.content: Title slide: SAGES Video Educational Offerings in MIS Surgery - shows educational resource materials and certification logos
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Introduction to FLS program and associated educational resources

frame_analyses.content: FLS Program Components - Web-based multimedia study guide, Laparoscopic Trainer Box, 2-part proctored assessment
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Overview of FLS curriculum structure and components

frame_analyses.content: FLS Certification requirements and American Board of Surgery mandate (2009-2010)
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Context of FLS in surgical residency training requirements

frame_analyses.content: Incorporation of FLS into residency - objectives for assessment and training
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Educational goals: proficiency-based training, skill retention, clinical readiness

frame_analyses.content: What is Perfect Practice - Ericsson's deliberate practice framework with 4 components
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Learning science: well-defined goals, motivation, feedback, repetition with refinement
Rationale for proficiency targets vs traditional time-based training

frame_analyses.content: Modified Expert-derived metrics table - task proficiency targets and allowable errors
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Specific FLS pass criteria: Task 1 (48s, no drops), Task 2 (98s, ±2mm), Task 3 (53s, ±1mm), Task 4 (136s, ±1mm gap), Task 5 (112s, no avulsion)

frame_analyses.content: Graph showing proficiency-based training improves pass rates - 21 medical students, all pass after independent practice
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Evidence that structured proficiency training with deliberate practice leads to 100% pass rates

frame_analyses.content: Benefits of overtraining on retention - plateau effect and skill consolidation
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Training above pass score improves long-term retention and skill plateau stability

frame_analyses.content: Proficiency targets increase motivation - comparison of practice volume in goal vs non-goal group
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Behavioral science: explicit proficiency targets drive more practice repetitions (62 vs 42 LapSim reps)

frame_analyses.content: Training schedule: Massed vs Distributed practice - advantages of distributed practice
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Pedagogical principle: spaced practice improves retention vs massed practice

frame_analyses.content: FLS simulator training improves early clinical performance - RCT showing 54% GRS improvement vs 15% control
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Clinical validation: FLS training translates to better operative room performance

frame_analyses.content: McGill institutional implementation - simulation center, faculty resources, protected teaching time
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Program infrastructure and institutional support for FLS training

frame_analyses.content: Challenges: resident variability, proctored practice logistics, independent practice access, cost
McGill FLS Training protocol - July course, didactic, 6 stations, proficiency assessment, log book
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Specific institutional curriculum structure and assessment schedule

frame_analyses.content: McGill distributed training model - PGY1 July/August, 5-12 hours total, Wednesday PM sessions, key card access
frame_analyses.teaching_content: Implementation of distributed practice model with flexible independent practice

frame_analyses.content: McGill FLS testing - appointment-based testing during PGY4 year, 100% pass rate

video_summary: This is an educational presentation about FLS program design, implementation, and proficiency-based training at McGill University. The video contains no performance task footage — it is didactic material covering: (1) FLS program structure and certification requirements, (2) learning science principles (deliberate practice, proficiency-based vs experience-based training), (3) research evidence on efficacy, (4) institutional implementation strategy, and (5) challenges and solutions.

verbal_cues: Proficiency targets increase motivation and practice volume
Distributed practice improves retention vs massed practice
Training above pass score creates skill plateau and long-term retention
Explicit proficiency goals drive more independent repetitions
FLS simulator training correlates with improved OR performance
common_errors_addressed: Experience-based (time/volume) training does not account for individual learning curves
Massed practice (intensive single session) leads to worse long-term retention than distributed practice
Training to pass score alone does not optimize retention — overtraining to plateau improves skill stability
teaching_content.target_audience: Surgical educators, program directors, faculty implementing FLS training

scoring_note: Not applicable — this video contains no FLS task performance footage. No trainee or surgeon executes any FLS task (Peg Transfer, Pattern Cut, Endoloop, Extracorporeal Suture, or Intracorporeal Suture). Video is purely educational/instructional content about FLS program design and evidence-based training principles.
confidence_rationale: Content is clearly an academic presentation with slides, text overlays, and educational diagrams. No task performance is shown.
